data.title: Spotify: YouTube Exposes A Valuation Built On Sand
data.description:
Backdrop and Positives

Since my first Spotify (NYSE:SPOT) coverage over a year ago, the stock has surged 120% - quite the opposite of my unmistakably bearish stance on it as an investment. With that, I congratulate all investors for their gains and want to review if parts of my thesis were flawed. Let us briefly go over some key aspects of last year's thesis:

"In the short term, momentum is on the side of Spotify amid increasing profitability." - While this was basically correct, I underestimated the extent to which investors would react to the (already previously anticipated) company's turn to profitability.

"My long-term sell rating only applies to those who share my diagnosis about the business model." - One year is not the "long-term."

Therefore, my reasoning for why my bearishness about Spotify stock has only solidified as the share price climbed over the past year is as follows:

  1. YouTube has the superior relative value proposition, which might not lead to Spotify users switching but limit its growth as YouTube Music is gaining significant market share.
  2. This risk is not reflected in Spotify's premium valuation.

But first, honor where honor is due. As the music-streaming first-mover, Spotify still benefits from serious lock-in effects of existing users "due to habit, familiarity, and the playlists users have saved." It is the market leader, and its balance sheet consists of 50% cash & equivalents and short-term investments, backed by 49% in recorded equity.

No Competitive Edge Against YouTube

When looking at subscriber numbers below, it becomes clear that Spotify is still a synonym for the music streaming industry. Competitors like those from other big tech players like Apple Music (AAPL) or Amazon Music (AMZN) also grow and make up some market share, but so far, they have not become as relevant as one might have expected.

Still, there is a steadily rising yellow line in the chart below that I believe seriously makes life more difficult for Spotify: YouTube (GOOGL).

Sticking to these top 7 global music streamers, Spotify's 4-year subscriber CAGR was 16%, below that of the aggregate top 7 of 20% and significantly lower than YouTube's 43%. As a result, Spotify's 43% subscriber share among the top 7 in 2020 has come down to 36% in 2024, while YouTube doubled its share from 9% to 18%. Refer below for YoY growth rates over the last four years.

But that is not yet the most pressing issue, which could be what the next chart reveals. Out of its almost 700 million users, 276 million (40%; 2024: 39%) are premium subscribers. Spotify's 40% (2024: 39%) premium share from overall users is among the highest in the industry already, apart from Apple, which, according to these data points, has a higher subscription count than actual users (probably due to bundling and Apple users not even using Apple Music).

So, while there is still theoretical monetization upside of 60%, plus overall user and industry growth, the chart below makes it only logical to assume that YouTube is better positioned. Out of 2.2 billion YouTube users, only 125 million (6%) were paying subscribers as of 2024, offering 94% premium-monetization headroom from existing users alone. Add to that the fact that YouTube is simultaneously disrupting TV and video consumption as a whole.

Next, it does not make a lot of sense to pay €12.99 (where I live) for Spotify when you can have YouTube Music, including all of YouTube Premium video content, for the exact same price tag. The value-for-money comparison is a no-brainer, from my perspective. And Spotify seems to need those prices to fulfill shareholders fantasies, while YouTube - part of Alphabet - is definitely better equipped to sit out price differentials if they wanted to gain subscribers at an even faster rate.

But then, of course, Spotify also makes money off of non-premium users through ads. At the end of the day, however, the only really needle-moving tier for Spotify is premium, as the charts below clearly show. This becomes even more apparent when looking at gross profit contribution in the second picture on the left.

Valuation Reiterates Sell

Finally, I concluded last year that a 2027 FWD P/E of 34 did not adequately account for the risks I laid out. Today, the 2027 FWD P/E is an even higher 39. But I do want to acknowledge that Spotify has always had free cash flow far exceeding reported earnings.

Free cash flow margin has been in the low single digits for the better part of the late 2010s and early 2020s but has gradually increased to a TTM figure of 17% and is expected to further climb to 19% by 2027. For reference, Netflix also yields FCF margins around the 20% mark, so I do not want to become any more optimistic about these for Spotify either.

As a result, the 2027 expected FCF of $5.1 billion represents a FWD FCF yield of 3.7% against Spotify's current enterprise value of $137 billion, equating to a 27x FWD EV/FCF multiple for more than 2 years out. This over 2-year forward yield (multiple) appears way too low (high) to adequately account for the competitive risks laid out in this article. I therefore stick to my Sell rating on Spotify.

Takeaway

YouTube's 2.2 billion users represent huge monetization potential for YouTube Music, which currently only counts 125 million subscribers (6%). As such, and given YouTube's superior value proposition, I expect YouTube Music to continue to gain market share. Current growth rates already prove this. From my perspective, Spotify's USP remains too weak for the long term. Against this risky backdrop, a 2027 FWD P/E of 39 appears too high. Therefore, I reiterate my Sell rating on Spotify.

data.title: Energy Dividend Stocks: I Like Kinder Morgan, But Love Enterprise Products Partners
data.description:
EPD And KMI Stock: Latest Dividends In Focus

My last coverage on Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD) was published on Aug 29 under a title of "Enterprise Products Partners: Insiders Bought More In July, So Did I". As stated in the title, that analysis was triggered by EPD's insider purchase, and I rated the stock as a strong buy. As for Kinder Morgan, Inc. (NYSE:KMI), my last analysis was published on Aug 22 in an article entitled " Kinder Morgan Is A Better Energy Bet Than XLE". The article was triggered by my outlook for oil prices given the geopolitical developments, mostly the OPEC+ production decision and tariff/trade tension, at that time. The article ended with a buy rating for KMI, as I consider it to have superior ROCE and growth prospects than the average energy sector represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE).

Given the pronounced role of dividends for energy stocks, especially storage and transportation stocks like KMI and EPD, a material development on my radar was their latest dividend declarations, as shown in the chart below. For Enterprise Products Partners, the company's latest declaration is a regular quarterly cash dividend of $0.5450 per share, with an Ex-Div date of 10/31/2025. This latest dividend marks a year-over-year growth of +1.87%. Similarly, Kinder Morgan also declared a regular quarterly dividend recently in the amount of $0.2925 per share with an Ex-Div Date of 7/31/2025. This declaration reflects a year-over-year growth of +1.74%, slightly lower than EPD's growth.

These declarations, when combined with the stock price changes since my last writing, have made EPD's yield far more attractive than KMI's, both by horizontal and vertical comparisons. To wit, the chart below shows their forward dividend yield and also the four-year average yield. As seen, Enterprise currently has an FWD yield of 7.05% with its latest declaration, substantially above Kinder Morgan's FWD yield of 4.28%. By vertical comparison, EPD's current FWD yield is only 2% below its 4 Year Average Yield of 7.18%, suggesting an almost fair valuation. While in contrast, KMI's current yield is significantly lower than its 4 Year Average Yield of 5.70% (by about 25%).

Of course, dividend investors should be aware of the so-called dividend trap - stocks with higher yield but weaker fundamentals. As such, the subsequent sections of this article will perform a holistic analysis of EPD and KMI's dividend safety. The aspects covered and my conclusions are summarized in the scorecard below. My takeaway is that EPD does not only yield more, but also features better consistency, coverage, and valuation after adjusting for growth and yield.

EPD And KMI: Dividend Consistency And Growth

The rest of this article follows a framework that we use in our routine screening of dividend stocks. The frame is based on a collection of checkpoints we gathered while studying Peter Lynch's writings. The approach was thoroughly detailed in our earlier articles. As such, this article will only quote the most relevant parts as it proceeds. The framework, with no surprise, begins with establishing a track record of dividend growth, as quoted below:

Dividend Track Record: A long history of consistently increasing dividends is highly valued by Lynch. His guides are to look for companies with a track record of stable dividends for at least 20 years.

EPD is a dividend champion with 26 years of consecutive dividend growth, as seen in the table below. In contrast, KMI's current dividend growth started 7 years ago (although the company has had many more years of payouts). As such, we consider EPD's dividend record to be far stronger, which is also supported by its well-diversified profit streams. In our assessment, EPD is one of the best diversified players in the midstream space, both geographically and operationally. It is involved in almost every shale play and has significant operations in almost every segment of the midstream industry. As such, EPD has exhibited far less volatility than almost all of its peers, including KMI. For instance, during the last major economic crisis caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, EPD kept growing its payout distributions, while most players in the industry were forced to cut or eliminate their payouts.

Also note that EPD's payout ratio is 79.48%, lower than KMI's 97% by a good margin. Finally, before leaving this section, also note that EPD has been growing its dividends at faster rates in recent years, as seen, and I will detail the implications of this factor on its valuation.

EPD And KMI: Valuation

The next checkpoint in our framework is valuation assessment. For stocks like EPD and KMI, dividend yields represent a large portion of their total return potential. To factor this into the valuation equation, Lynch's insight is to use the following PEGY ratio:

PEG refers to the price-to-earnings growth ratio. He also introduced a modified version of the PEG ratio for dividends stocks. The modified ratio is called the PEGY ratio and is computed as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The concept is simple yet effective. If the dividends are high enough, investors should not expect (and do not need either) a high growth rate anymore. Just like the PEG ratio, Lynch favors PEGY ratios below 1x.

With this insight, the table displays my estimates for KMI and EPD's PEGY ratios. Their FY1 P/Es were computed with the share prices as of this writing and the latest consensus EPS estimates. For the growth rate projection, I simply assumed an extrapolation of their dividend growth rate in the past 5 years, which was 3.78% for EPD and 2.51% for KMI, as you can see from the previous chart. As for yield, I plugged in their FWD dividend yields as mentioned in the first section. With these assumption, KMI's PEGY ratio worked out to 3.51x, significantly above EPD's 1.09x (which is also quite close to the ideal threshold of 1x).

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of downside risks, investment in either EPD or KMI entails a common set of risks generic to the overall energy sector. These risks include but are not limited to policy/regulation risks, geopolitical risks, the secular shift away from fossil fuels, etc. Here, I will concentrate more on the risks that are directly relevant to the focus of this analysis, which is dividend safety and sustainability.

We commented that EPD has a better payout ratio compared to KMI. However, payout ratio is only one aspect of dividend safety. Especially for stocks like EPD and KMI, MLP investors must be aware of the leverage risks behind their high-yielding shares. In this debt-heavy sub-sector, most stocks (both EPD and KMI included) rely on debt-financing both more than the average economy and also other sub-sectors in the energy industry. As an example, based on their balance sheet data below, EPD and KMI exhibit similar levels - and both are elevated compared to other sectors in my view - of financial leverage. EPD's Total Debt to Equity ratio stands at 110%, and KMI's ratio is quite similar at 102%. In terms of Debt/Total Capital ratio, EPD's reading is 49.81%, again very similar to KMI's 49.29%.

Finally, profitability is the last checkpoint in our framework, a checkpoint that most dividend investors overlook because profitability is not as directly related to dividends as payout ratios and/or dividend growth rates. However, thicker profit margins do translate into better downside protection, especially for energy stocks that are notorious for their cyclicality and volatility. On this front, KMI is definitely the winner, as you can easily tell from the following screenshot. KMI enjoys far better margins than EPD on all metrics across the income statement, as seen.

To close, this revisit to KMI and EPD was prompted by their latest dividend declarations. These declarations have shown YOY dividend growth rates noticeably below the average rates in the past 3 or 5 years, signaling the profit pressure facing the sector in the near term. To better illustrate the point, our market dashboard below shows that the overall energy sector, represented by XLE, suffered a dividend decrease of 3.2% YOY on a TTM basis. As such, even though we are attracted to the valuation of this sector amid an otherwise expensive market, we believe investors do need to exercise discretion. We consider both EPD and KMI to be stronger than the average sector for reasons detailed in our past article and also their latest dividend growth. And a closer examination shows EPD to be an even better-rounded package.

data.title: Brighthouse Financial: Undervalued Insurer Poised For A Rebound
data.description:
Brighthouse Financial (NASDAQ:BHF) has gone through a sizeable transition.

The company was formed as a spin-off from MetLife in 2017 and is now regarded as a key player in the annuity and life insurance space.

In 2024, BHF reported record-breaking sales, and that growth has carried into 2025 with record sales in Q2 2025.

This stock is capturing quite the attention.

And with the stock trading at an adjusted price-to-adjusted-book value ratio of 0.33, it signals a rare value opportunity.

Here’s why this under-the-radar stock deserves a closer look.

Why BHF Is Attracting Attention

The spin-off from MetLife back in 2017 allowed the company to become a pure play in the annuities and life insurance sector of the market.

The shift seemed like a no brainer. BHF becomes a go-to stock for those chasing income in the market.

And with inflation roaring up in recent years, retirements getting extended, and boomers dumping cash into annuity plans, the industry has seen a bit of a boom.

Sales hit a record in 2024, and BHF is still seeing growth.

In 2025, annuity sales reached $2.3 billion in Q1 (led by $2.0 billion in Shield products) and $2.6 billion in Q2 (up 8% year-over-year and 16% sequentially).

Needless to say, this is a stock that gets my attention.

And as you view their financials and key ratios, one metric reels you in - Adjusted Book Value Per Share (excluding accumulated other comprehensive income).

Their adjusted book value per share came in at $144.09 based on Q2 2025 numbers. With the stock around $48, we're talking about a price-to-adjusted-book ratio of about 0.33.

That's absurdly cheap.

Even when looking at its unadjusted price-to-book ratio, it sits at 0.5, implying the stock is valued at roughly 50 cents for every dollar of its net assets.

Stacking Up Against The Competition

Let's put that in context with the big boys.

Prudential (PRU) trades at around 0.8 on its adjusted book value, even with similar annuity exposure. Lincoln National (LNC) is closer to 0.6, but still a hefty premium over BHF.

That begs the question: Why the massive discount for BHF?

You could blame it on spin-off hangover and some short-term earnings noise from mortality bumps. But with rock-solid capital ratios in the 405-425% range and that adjusted book screaming value, this gap won't last.

If BHF narrows this ratio even half of the spread, you're looking at a 30-50% plus upside from here.

Recent Developments: Buyout Buzz And Capital Firepower

The big news lately was takeover chatter in late September.

Aquarian Holdings reportedly floated a $65-$70 per share offer, sending shares rocketing 27% on September 22, to $57.59.

Nothing's official yet, but Raymond James slapped a Strong Buy on the stock with a $72 target on September 29, citing the deal potential.

Sure, the stock's pulled back since. That's thanks to broader market jitters and rate cut worries.

But, in my opinion, that just makes it a better entry.

Analysts like Keefe, Bruyette & Woods trimmed to $50 on October 9, and Weiss kept a Hold (C-) on October 14. The consensus still hovers around $56-$58 per share, implying solid conviction in the investment community.

On top of that, BHF's has been pushing buybacks. In Q2 alone, the company bought back over $43 million in shares bringing the total year-to-date amount to $102 million.

Other Upside Drivers

While that pretty much sums up the key reasons to follow the stock and jump in after the recent selloff, there are other drivers of upside momentum.

For example, the demographics are a goldmine for insurance companies. An aging community is in need of income security and BHF's fixed annuities and Shield products are perfect for it. They've dialed in hedging to tame volatility from variable annuities, which they touted in the Q2 call, aiming to keep results steady even if markets wobble.

The M&A angle remains in play too.

Beyond Aquarian, BHF's balance sheet makes it a continued target for anyone looking to get into the annuity space or looking to expand their book of business.

As a CMT (Chartered Market Technician), I can't skip the charts.

BHF's been coiling in a $20 range, from roughly $40 up to $60 per share, for years now. The stock has been bouncing back and forth around the 200-day simple moving average. Now, our basic takeaway on this is the longer the consolidation, the bigger the breakout. Which is what we are watching today.

With BHF primed for a breakout in the coming weeks, following the underlying technical indicators can tip us off to the big move.

Right now, RSI's neutral, MACD's flirting with a bullish cross (which speaks to the dip buying opportunity), and volume's been picking up in the first 10 months of 2025.

After missing analyst estimates on earnings and revenue in Q2 2025, a solid earnings beat could send the stock through $50, eyeing $55-$60 fast.

Downside Risks

Of course, no investment or trade thesis is bullet proof.

Earnings missing analyst expectations from Q2 could be the start of a trend for the stock where it reports weaker earnings throughout 2025.

BHF has plenty of room for shortfalls to develop, and we don't want to write that off. The Fed may cut rates more aggressively than expected, growth could slow for various reasons, or life expectancy could get an extension. These are all things that would more than likely have a negative effect on the top and bottom line for BHF.

Not to mention the economy.

There are rumblings of a potential stalled growth on various levels of the U.S. economy right now. The Fed is taking center stage again, but we also have worries about bad debt in regional banks, weaker job growth, trade war with China, and tariff-related headwinds still linger.

We just pointed out in the technicals that the price range to watch is $40-$60. If any of these events cause a selloff below $40, the downside risk is just getting started.

Wrapping It Up: Time To Buy The Dip

Look, BHF isn't going to wow you with hype. We know it is not a tech play, and it is not chasing cryptocurrency-type moves.

But it is cheap. And when looking at key metrics like its adjusted book, along with continued buyout whispers and annuity momentum, that makes BHF a solid target here for value hunters.

As always, do your own digging, but this is one undervalued stock in my book.

data.title: Rethinking Safe Havens: Exploring Euro And Sterling Bonds Amid U.S. Uncertainty
data.description:
By Tzvetelina Georgieva

For many years, global issuers have gravitated toward the U.S. dollar bond market, drawn by its unparalleled liquidity, vast investor base and efficient access to funding. However, recent developments in the U.S. yield environment are beginning to shift perspectives on the U.S. Treasury market’s long-held reputation as the world financial safe haven. In response, market participants are increasingly turning to other regions, including the euro and sterling bond markets, which are gaining traction as alternatives for market participants seeking stability and value in today’s shifting landscape.

The euro-denominated government and corporate bond markets, anchored by the relative stability and scale of the eurozone, continue to offer robust liquidity and diversification opportunities. Similarly, the U.K.'s sterling bond market, supported by its long-standing reputation for transparency and regulatory strength, is drawing renewed interest amid global volatility. This is due to the relative value and resilience found within these European markets, with both GBP and EUR bonds providing options for those seeking to navigate the evolving global fixed income landscape. This shift in focus underscores the growing importance of European currencies in global portfolios, as market participants seek to balance risk and performance in an environment marked by fiscal uncertainty in the U.S.

During the period from April 1, 2025, to Aug. 28, 2025, U.S. Treasuries (as represented by the iBoxx $ Treasuries) saw a modest performance, posting a return of 1.13%. In comparison, the iBoxx Global Government EMEA Index, which represents sovereign bond issues by central governments in the EMEA, recorded a gain of 1.66% for the same timeframe (see Exhibit 1), in USD-hedged terms. Individual sovereign indices covering German, French, Italian and British sovereign bonds also registered positive performance in USD-hedged terms. The iBoxx Global Government Italy rose by 2.04%, while the iBoxx Global Government Germany increased by 0.35% when compared to U.S. Treasuries.

Performance dynamics shift when accounting for the EUR-USD and GBP-USD exchange rates, as reflected in USD unhedged indices. Over the period, the iBoxx Global Government EMEA index outperformed the iBoxx $ Treasuries by 7.82% (see Exhibit 2). Additionally, when examining individual sovereign markets in USD unhedged terms, several of them posted notably higher returns compared to U.S. Treasuries. German sovereign bonds increased 7.29%, while Italian sovereign bonds gained 9.20% over the same period.

Shifting Correlations and Improved Diversification

The correlation data highlights a notable divergence between six-month (April 2025-August 2025) and 10-year relationships among U.S. Treasuries and major European sovereign bond markets. Over the April 2025-August 2025 period, correlations with U.S. Treasuries were significantly lower than their respective 10-year averages. For example, Germany and France, which both show strong 10-year correlations of 89% with U.S. Treasuries, had much weaker short-term correlations of 37% and 36%, respectively. Italy and the U.K. also display this pattern, with 10-year correlations of 74% and 76% that dropped to 45% and 31% in the recent period, respectively. The broader EMEA index follows suit, with a 10-year correlation of 62% but only 44% for the six-month period. This suggests that while U.S. and European government bonds had historically moved closely together, recent market conditions have led to greater differentiation and reduced co-movement, reflecting shifting investor sentiment and regional economic factors.

If sustained, the recent decline in correlation between U.S. Treasuries and European sovereign bonds highlights the potential for new diversification opportunities. Strong unhedged returns in EUR and GBP markets, especially Italian and German bonds, suggest growing investor interest. If U.S. fiscal uncertainty persists, European bonds might be an interesting alternative for investors looking for global diversification.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ServiceNow: A Great Business, But Valuation Needs To Cool Down -- A Hold
data.description:
ServiceNow, Inc. (NYSE:NOW) is an American software company. Now specializes in cloud-based solutions, allowing companies to optimize, digitize, and automate workflows. The company offers a user-friendly platform called Now Platform, which helps manage operations through a centralized and AI-tailored system.

The company trades at a forward P/E of 54.69, which indicates a roughly 2.16x premium versus the sector median of 25.22x. This implies a significant overvaluation, and I think the market may have run ahead of its fundamentals. The stock has declined by about 14% year-to-date, underlining a substantial market underperformance. I think the trend is likely to continue, as despite the selloff, it remains trading at elevated levels.

NOW appears to be an impressive top- and bottom-line growth story, operating with above-market margins. I think it could be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who favor AI exposure and high-growth names, but given its current valuation, the upside may be limited. I believe it may offer market-like returns, but I am not sure whether it could outperform the benchmark over the next 12 months.

I rate ServiceNow as a hold with a $1,000 price target over the next year. This would imply a 9% upside possibility. I think the stock may follow its bottom-line growth, but given an already significant premium, the upside may be capped. Regardless, this is a great business that is well-executed, but I think the valuations need to cool down to make it a more compelling long-term play.

FQ2 2025 Snapshot: Top And Bottom Line Growth Remains Intact

On July 23, the company reported its FQ2 2025 results. This was a strong, double-beat quarter, highlighting significant top- and bottom-line growth. The stock price has increased more than 4% over the following trading day.

The software company posted $3.22 billion in revenue, underscoring a 22% growth on a year-over-year basis, and $91 million above the analysts' estimates (2.8% surprise). The bottom-line came in at $4.09, up by about 33% from $3.13 in the same quarter last year, and $0.52 above the analysts' expectations (12.7% surprise).

In their presentation, management highlighted $3.11 billion in subscription revenue, up by 22% over the past 12 months. This is roughly in line with the 23% growth over the prior year, representing a continuation in strong demand. This is important, as this underlines a strong position in the industry and compelling services to its customers. I think this is also an important metric to monitor for long-term investors.

The company experienced a significant increase in the number of customers with more than $5 million in annual contract value. The number surged from 442 in FQ2 2024 to 528 in FQ2 2025, representing roughly a 20% increase over the past year. Management highlighted that the number of such customers has also increased by 4% on a quarter-over-quarter basis, which justifies my thesis that NOW's top-line growth remains intact.

ServiceNow has also maintained the renewal rate of 98% over the past year, indicating high-quality services offered and a sticky customer base, fueling the bullish narrative for the software company.

Management provided guidance for the upcoming quarter and the full year of 2025. ServiceNow anticipates about $3.26 billion in revenue, which would imply a 20% increase on a year-over-year basis. For FY2025, management aims for $12.785 billion in revenue at the midpoint, up by 20% on an annual basis. The company also expects a 30.5% non-GAAP operating margin for both the upcoming quarter and for the full year. This would support the bullish stance for the company, and may result in the stock sustaining its premium valuation.

Although I think the company appears to be well-positioned to achieve the guided results, I believe the market has already priced that in. This is why I think investors could have run ahead of fundamentals, and I would not be surprised if the company continues to consolidate. Regardless, ServiceNow remains a robust business with a sticky customer base and a high-demand product. Its top and bottom line growth remains intact.

ServiceNow Appears To Be Expensive, And That May Weigh On The Stock Price In The Near-Term

NOW stock trades at a forward P/E of about 50x, which highlights more than 2x premium versus the average company in the S&P 500 at about a 22.76x earnings multiple. This is significant, and premium valuation may limit the upside in the near term. However, the below chart indicates that the stock has been trading at a substantial premium for years, and if management continues to execute well, NOW may continue trading at such a multiple.

The forward PEG (non-GAAP) at 1.77 offsets the overvalued narrative and implies the stock trades at about a 2.50% discount to peers versus the sector median of 1.81. This is interesting, as it highlights that if the company can continue top- and bottom-line growth at such a phenomenal pace, the market may be willing to reward the stock with a higher premium. This also justifies the data provided above, supporting the thesis of multiple expansion, as NOW traded at above 75x earnings multiple back in January 2025.

Nevertheless, the forward EV/Sales and Price/Sales at 13.91 and 14.55, respectively, balance the bullish outlook and indicate that the market has already priced in the future growth to a large extent. These valuation metrics represent roughly 4x overvaluation at the midpoint compared to the sector median of 3.48 and 3.45. Therefore, although the company could proceed with phenomenal business expansion further, it may not necessarily translate to multiple expansions.

On the upside, ServiceNow appears to be a low-leverage business with total obligations of $2.41 billion. This highlights financial flexibility for management to use debt instruments, if needed. The company has $6.13 billion in cash that could be used to pay the debt with ease.

The current high-interest rate environment appears to be favorable for NOW. Last quarter, the software company experienced a positive change in net interest income of $110 million. This appears to be a tailwind for the bottom-line growth, as long as the Fed maintains an elevated interest rate policy.

ServiceNow operates in the enterprise software market. It has been valued at roughly $264 billion in 2024, and is expected to nearly double in size over the next 5 years to $517 billion by 2030. This indicates a 12.10% CAGR. Considering the current demand for AI-tailored solutions, the ServiceNow platform may remain favorable in the years ahead. The enterprise software market is expected to significantly increase in the upcoming years, and NOW appears to be well-positioned to benefit from its business optimization segment.

The software company grew its revenue by 21% over the past 12 months, and that is another strong argument supporting the upside. This also indicates a nearly 3x advantage versus the sector median of about 7%. The forward revenue growth rate (TTM) of 20.41% supports the continuation and falls in line with both the company's guidance and my thesis of the enterprise software landscape expansion.

NOW grew its bottom line by 43% over the past year, which represents 4.8x outperformance versus peers of 9%. The company achieved 7% CapEx growth over the past 12 months, supporting continued investments into its business, which could significantly contribute to future top-line growth.

ServiceNow operates at above-market margins, which makes it a compelling opportunity to consider. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 78% appears to be more than 59% higher than that of peers, which is 49%. The net income margin (TTM) of 13% highlights about 3x outperformance compared to the sector median of 4.59%. The software companies are known for significant margins, but these results are phenomenal, highlighting that NOW trades deservedly at a premium.

The company generated an impressive $4.7 billion in cash from operations (TTM), more than 38x the advantage compared to peers of $121 million. This led to $15.05 in cash per share (TTM), underscoring about 7x outperformance versus the sector median of $2.17.

ServiceNow appears to be a high-quality business that has a sticky customer base. The company operates at high margins, which makes the business highly profitable. Its AI-tailored solutions support significant demand, and the enterprise software market growth estimates support the bullish outlook for the company.

Wall Street forecasts $16.89 in diluted EPS for FY2025, and $20.04 for FY2026. This would represent strong bottom-line growth at a double-digit pace. Considering a dominant stance in the industry and excellent execution by management, I think ServiceNow appears to be well-positioned to drive the AI-related wave and achieve the results.

I think that the earnings multiple may come down in the next year, but the stock price may mirror the bottom-line growth further. If we apply a 50x earnings multiple to an FY2026 EPS estimate of $20.04, we come in at about my price target of $1,000. The chart above supports my thesis, indicating that the stock price correlates with bottom-line growth. I think this trend is likely to continue, but I am not sure if it will result in significant multiple expansion and benchmark outperformance because of already elevated valuation.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to ServiceNow, which could dramatically impact the stock price in the near term.

The company trades at a significant premium to both the sector median and the average company in the S&P500. The broader market has rallied since the April lows, without experiencing a meaningful pullback. One could argue that the market may take a breather, which could result in NOW stock price decline. The company's valuation appears elevated, and if sentiment changes, investors may reduce exposure to expensive names.

NOW operates in a fiercely competitive landscape. There are more rivals who are competing for the market share, including Microsoft, Oracle, and Salesforce. AI-driven automation and workflow optimizations will remain a priority for thousands of businesses, as they could efficiently use corporate resources. Although I think that multiple businesses can successfully compete in the high-demand market, if NOW loses its market share, the stock could be repriced at a lower valuation.

A sentiment around ServiceNow is positive, and that has been a significant tailwind, driving the stock price higher. This applies to multiple AI-related names. However, if heavy investments in AI do not translate to meaningful top and bottom line growth for the companies, sentiment around AI could shift, and the stock market could correct. AI-related names would probably take the hardest hit, as investors would not be willing to price the stocks at a significant premium.

However, if none of the above materializes, I think the company could continue posting substantial results. Although valuation appears to be elevated, I think the NOW stock price could follow bottom-line performance. I am not sure whether it will be enough to outperform the benchmark, but I think the software company has more room to run.

Conclusion: A High-Quality Business, But Valuation Appears Too Pricey To Chase

I believe ServiceNow may appear to be an interesting opportunity for long-term investors who favor AI names and significant margins. The company is tied to a sticky customer base and continued top and bottom line growth. However, the stock trades at a substantial premium, indicating that the market knows that NOW is a phenomenal business, which may limit the upside in the near term.

Based on my analysis, I think that the stock price could appreciate further if management can continue growing the bottom line at such a rapid pace. Historically, the stock price performance correlated with EPS growth, and I think the trend is likely to continue. However, at the current earnings multiple, I believe it could be challenging for ServiceNow to outperform the benchmark.

This is why I rate ServiceNow a hold with a $1,000 price target over the next 12 months. This underscores 10% upside, which may represent market-like performance. 44 Wall Street analysts forecast a $1,147 12-month price target and roughly 26% upside. This is definitely a possibility if sentiment remains elevated, but I favor a more conservative approach, which leaves me room for error.

data.title: Apartment Investment & Management Company: Unlocking A Concentrated Value Opportunity
data.description:
Apartment Investment & Management Company (NYSE:AIV) has not delivered the pivotal year hoped for when last covered on Seeking Alpha, generating negative YTD returns amidst rallies in U.S. equity indices. However, I believe their recent moves signal a willingness to fully liquidate their portfolio, which does not appear to be priced into the stock.

The Background

AIV owns the remaining assets from the AIRC spin-off in 2020 (then ~90% of their combined value), which went on to be acquired by Blackstone (BX) last year for about $10B. The REIT struggled to find natural owners without a dividend, with development assets that are notoriously hard to value. The stock has ping-ponged between $5 and $10, with corporate buybacks unable to close what management described as a significant discount to fair value. Ultimately, AIV has decided to pursue “alternatives” at the beginning of 2025, and this pursuit is ongoing.

The Catalyst

AIV recently closed on the sale of their suburban Boston properties, so far declaring a $2.23 per share special cash dividend and closing their final sale in Q4. This was in addition to the company's previously announced sale of two assets in Miami’s Brickell neighborhood for $520m, which remains on track to close in Q4-25.

The combined proceeds from all scheduled dispositions, including the Boston and Brickell sales, are expected to total $1.26B and generate approximately $785m in net proceeds after retiring associated debt. Management has committed to returning between $4-4.20 per share to stockholders from the total net proceeds, inclusive of the dividend mentioned above.

I believe the market is misinterpreting the Boston sale, and the stock sold off in its wake. First, these were very old properties with significant deferred maintenance, about an hour from Boston. Some were literally in New Hampshire. They were also sources of significant cash flow for the company, which was redeployed into development projects in the DC area and Florida. By selling their legacy cash-generating assets, the company is moving towards full liquidation, as their ability to self-fund development diminishes.

Estimating Remaining Value and Potential IRR

The primary value proposition for AIV lies in the significant discount between its share price and the Net Asset Value, or “NAV” of its properties. My assumptions for calculating the remaining NAV are as follows:

  • The company has already outlined expected Brickell/Boston proceeds treatment, including about $150m being withheld for corporate purposes and a $4-4.20 dividend.
  • Remaining Stabilized NOI: $51m, 6-6.5% aggregate cap rate expected offset by $430m recourse debt: $350-420m NAV

  • 2026 Stabilized NOI: $39m, 5.5-6% cap rate offset by $400m associated debt: $250-310m NAV
  • Miami 240 development carrying $165m debt: $35-50m NAV

  • $3m unconsolidated NOI: $40-50m NAV
  • Benson Hotel: $35-50m NAV
  • Remaining development properties & RE Tech assets: $150-200m NAV

  • Cash & notes receivable: I don’t believe the Brickell deposit cash is on the balance sheet already (so no double counting): $150m NAV
  • Total estimated NAV: $1,160 - $1,380m

Counting against these assets, we have:

  • $150m preferred stock liability
  • $150m other liabilities on balance sheet
  • Assume $50m liquidation/CIC costs
  • Net NAV: $810 - $1,030m

Added together, this gets you to a midpoint of $900m net distributable value over ~150m fully diluted shares (adjusting for GP conversion), or about $10-10.20/share before the expected dividends. Once the total cash distribution hits, the implied value of the remaining portfolio is deeply discounted relative to its estimated NAV:

  • Stock Price Before Distribution(s): $7.85/share
  • Total Expected Cash Return (Midpoint of $4.00-$4.20), $4.10/share
  • Author’s Estimated NAV (Pre-Distribution) $10/share
  • Estimated Remaining NAV (Post $4.10 Distribution): $5.90/share
  • Implied Price of Remaining Equity: $3.75/share
  • Potential Upside on Remaining Equity: 57%

I thought the stock price might recover more today after the first $2.23 dividend, but has traded down almost exactly in line with the payout, juicing the upside for new investors.

Risks

  • I may be wrong about the read-through from the recent Boston sale, with the remaining assets not being sold in the near term. I expect enough assets will be sold in the next twelve months to fully cover my cost basis.
  • Asset sale prices could underwhelm. AIV does not report asset management expenses in NOI, which has caused some confusion for investors on calculating cap rates on sale.
  • If the Brickell sale breaks, I would expect the stock price to decline. However, there were plenty of other rumored bidders on the site, and the non-refundable deposit should help bridge the gap to the next best bid.
  • For investors with taxable accounts, the dividends will be less efficient than those with tax-deferred retirement accounts. This likely limits the universe of investors considering AIV.
  • Rising interest rates could significantly impact the value of AIV's properties, whereas falling rates should present an opportunity for more upside than what I have shown here.

Conclusion

AIV looks attractive to me after recent sale announcements of their Boston portfolio and Brickell Assemblage. With one major dividend already paid out, the go-forward risk reward is looking significantly more attractive. I expect future asset sales announcements to cover my cost basis, with upside coming from sales of their remaining development projects. Disagree? Let me know why in the comments.

data.title: Rates Spark: Gilts Gaining Confidence
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

US regional banking concerns spark renewed flight to safety

10y US Treasury yields dipped below 4% on Thursday. Also, 2y yields have slid to below 3.4%, falling beneath the levels just after 'Liberation Day,' hinting at growing expectations that the Fed might have to take rates below the neutral level. The trigger for the latest rates rally is concerns about hidden credit risks within the US regional banking sector; the equities index for the sector slid 6%, although it is not clear at this stage to what degree this is a broader systemic issue or just markets jumping on headlines for lack of other drivers.

Over the past few weeks, the US shutdown has already weighed on sentiment, especially given the stories about larger layoffs in the federal workforce amid general worries about the job market. On the other hand, the shutdown means there were not many big directional impulses from the data side. So what had been missing was a good reason to get below 4% in the 10y, but concerns surrounding regional banks are now the new input.

Apart from the downward drift also to EUR rates, the renewed flight to quality has clear implications for the German Bund, which is proving its role as a safe haven with yields dipping further below swaps.

Gilts winning this week reflecting confidence in November budget

The big winner this week is gilts, with the 10Y yield down some 20bp, but with the autumn budget on our doorstep, we should brace for more volatility. Whilst gilts are strongly correlated with US Treasuries, they haven't followed the drift lower in US yields of the past few months. Instead, they remain stubbornly anchored around 4.6%. One key reason is that the Bank of England, unlike the Fed, is not in full cutting mode.

Another reason for still elevated yields is the risk premium embedded in gilts, but we think this should ease after we have more budget certainty. The spread between gilt yields and the swap rate currently stands at 62bp, which is now above the UST swap spread of 48bp. More surprisingly, gilt spreads have most recently risen in line with the widening of French government bond spreads but did not tighten alongside UST and Bunds. In fact, over the past year, gilt spreads were much more correlated with Italian government bond spreads than with US Treasuries or Bunds.

Whilst we acknowledge that the UK is facing fiscal challenges, we do think gilt yields should find themselves lower over the medium term. Our estimate of a 20bp risk premium versus 10Y US Treasuries still seems excessive, especially given that US government finances are under significant pressure. Once we get more certainty about the budget in November, the volatility in gilts should ease, which would then help the risk premium compress. We therefore see this week's decline in yields as a justified move, and there's still room for more.

Gilts were recently more aligned with French than German bonds

Friday’s events and market views

A light day in terms of data. From the eurozone, we only expect final CPI numbers for September, but these don't tend to change much. And due to the government shutdown, we don't anticipate any notable data from the US. In terms of central bank speakers, we have the Bank of England's Pill, Greene and Breeden talking later. From the European Central Bank, we have Nagel and Rehn, and from the Fed, Musalem.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: CareCloud 8.75% CUM RED PERP PFD SER B: Not Your Usual Preferred Shares
data.description:
Thesis

Preferred shares are usually considered fixed income instruments, although they do not sit on the 'Liabilities' part of the balance sheet. Investors use these capital markets instruments for income, with little upside present in the instruments. In most cases preferred shares are driven by interest rate risk, but occasionally they also see a significant impact from credit risk. It is the case for the CareCloud 8.75% Series B Preferred Shares (NASDAQ:CCLDO), which are preferred shares issued by CareCloud Inc. (NASDAQ:CCLD).

In today's article we are going to explore CCLD's capital structure and financials, and show investors why CCLDO are not your usual fixed income instrument, but rather represents a take on the company's future and profitability.

What does CareCloud do?

CareCloud is a healthcare technology company that provides a suite of cloud-based, technology-enabled solutions for medical practices and health systems. The company acts as a back office for healthcare businesses by handling billing and collections, managing patient data, and managing day to day scheduling and other tasks.

The company is fairly small, with a market capitalization of $135 million and total assets on the balance sheet of only $75 million.

Financial history

Despite its small size, the name has seen a fairly robust increase in revenue figures throughout time:

After a significant growth from 2019 to 2022, revenues have stagnated or even receded. The company is trying to rectify that via its Medsphere Systems Corporation acquisition, which just closed in August 2025. This acquisition is meant to expand CareCloud's reach into the hospital IT market, particularly for small and mid-sized hospitals.

The company's net income is again turning positive, after a number of years of negative figures:

More importantly, the management team's guidance is encouraging after its strategic acquisition:

That being said, there is another large reason for the positive outlook - the reduction in dividends from paying preferred shares. While the company has virtually no liabilities, it does spend roughly $12 million per year in dividend payments to preferred shares. That is why CCLD retired this year its Series A preferred shares via a conversion to common stock:

SOMERSET, N.J., March 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- CareCloud, Inc. (the “Company”) (Nasdaq: CCLD, CCLDO, CCLDP), a leader in healthcare information technology and generative AI solutions for medical practices and health systems nationwide, announced today (the “Mandatory Exchange Date”) that it effected the mandatory conversion (the "Conversion") of shares of its 8.75% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (the “Preferred Stock”), into the Company’s Common Stock, $0.001 par value per share (the “Common Stock”). “We are excited to announce this conversion, which will eliminate approximately $7 million or more in annual dividend obligations, freeing us to reinvest this capital in our growth,” said Norman Roth, Interim Chief Financial Officer and Corporate Controller of CareCloud.

We can see the immediate impact to net income in the latest quarter:

For the three months ended June 30, 2025 the preferred stock dividend moved to $1.3 million from $3.9 million in the same period in 2024.

Not traditional fixed income

As we have said before, traditional preferred equity investments rarely experience very substantial drawdowns, unless the underlying company is about to default. CCLDO however, has performed differently due to several factors:

  • small company with less financial flexibility
  • absence of other significant liabilities, with capital coming from preferred equity and common equity only
  • lack of a history of constant profitability

If we have a look at CCLDO's performance we will understand exactly what that means:

The preferred shares had a -85% drawdown in December 2023 when the company decided to suspend interest payment on the shares due to poor operating results. Furthermore the company took a $42 million goodwill impairment charge for that quarter, which moved its net income to deep negative territory.

Please note the correlation between the common shares and the preferred shares:

We rarely see such correlation, with the common equity being the first loss piece in a medium sized company. Due to size though, both the common and preferred shares move together when net income figures are below expectations. This should not occur, and does not occur, for a larger more established enterprise.

Yield considerations

The Series B comes with a coupon of 8.75%, but its current yield is 10%. We think this is too low. Fixed income instruments do not have -85% drawdowns without some sort of restructuring, and the fact that the upside is capped here but the downside is the same as common stock should command a higher coupon in our view.

When one invests in preferred equity one gets paid a coupon. The upside is limited by the $25/share par value, and even if the business does very well, all that benefit goes to common shareholders. Given the risk/rewards for CCLDO, one would expect to see current yields closer to 15%-20%, similar to what an investor gets via CLO equity CEFs. As we have seen from its historic performance, CCLDO acts like pure equity; thus, it should be priced as such.

We are of the opinion that investors looking for upside should buy the CCLD common equity, while traditional fixed income investors should look for either higher yields from CCLDO or avoid it altogether. CCLDO is not your typical fixed income investment.

Conclusion

CCLDO represents the Series B preferred shares from CareCloud Inc. The company is very small, with a market cap below $150 million, and has transformed its Series A preferred shares into common equity earlier in 2025. CCLDO had a -85% drawdown in 2023/2024 as CareCloud was not performing in line with expectations. The preferred shares are not a traditional fixed income investment, but represent more of a bet on the survival of the business, but without any of the upside of the common equity. Investors looking at CareCloud should look at the common stock, while traditional fixed income investors would be well-served by avoiding this volatile security.

data.title: Ryman Hospitality Properties: Upscale Hotels And Entertainment Assets
data.description:
Ryman Hospitality Properties (NYSE:RHP) ownership of upscale and meeting-focused destination hotels in a range of urban and resort markets in the U.S. has provided the REIT with healthy earnings visibility and a clear pathway for dividend growth as the Fed looks set to push through another 50 basis points of rate cuts to end 2025. However, while I've been adding more shares, near-term volatility is to be expected as elevated inflation expectations keep the yield on longer-term Treasuries high to dampen demand for REITs that are broadly now trading closer to their 52-week low. I'm in pursuit of income, with RHP last declaring a quarterly cash dividend of $1.15 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $4.6 per share annualized for a 5.09% dividend yield. This means a spread of around 111 basis points above the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). RHP's credit rating was most recently affirmed at "BB-" by Fitch Ratings. The play here is that RHP should be able to maintain a 4% to 6% compound annual growth rate far into the future, and that the REIT should see valuation eventually as the Fed's rate-cutting cycle is enacted. I last covered RHP last year, with the commons dipping since then in response to the Fed keeping rates broadly elevated up until the recent September rate cut.

Entertainment Growth, FFO Dip, And Dividend Coverage

RHP generated fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $659.51 million, up 7.5% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $41.15 million on consensus estimates. Growth was driven by the REIT's entertainment operating segment, Opry Entertainment Group's January acquisition of a majority interest in Southern Entertainment ("SE"). SE's portfolio includes a range of owned and promoted music festivals, including the Barefoot Country Music Fest in New Jersey's Wildwood, the Carolina Country Music Fest in Myrtle Beach, and an upcoming Field & Stream Music Fest in Winnsboro, South Carolina. However, the Field & Stream Music Fest was cancelled for the second year in a row, with full refunds being issued for tickets and other add-ons. The cancellation was likely due to low ticket sales. It represents a setback for RHP, as festivals are incredibly seasonal, and the 2025 Field & Stream Music Fest, slotted for the first week of October, would have acted to boost RHP's fourth-quarter earnings.

The REIT generated second-quarter funds from operations ("FFO") of $2.14 per share, down 43 cents from $2.57 a year ago, on the back of higher total operating expenses and interest expenses. Total operating expenses were up 16.8% year-over-year to $520 million, a rate of growth that came in ahead of total revenue growth. Food and beverage was the most disappointing line item, with a year-over-year decline in sales realized during the second quarter as its relevant expenses were up $3.8 million over the same time frame. Crucially, entertainment now forms 21.7% of total revenue, up 636 basis points from 15.36% in the year-ago period. This unit includes concerts, music-inspired restaurants, digital content, and the Grand Ole Opry, a live music venue in Nashville. The REIT is still covering its dividend by 186% through its second-quarter FFO and is guiding for adjusted FFO for its full-year fiscal 2025 to come in at $8.21 per share at the midpoint for end-of-year dividend coverage of around 178% on an adjusted basis.

RHP's acquisition of JW Marriott Phoenix Desert Ridge for $865 million is also set to be accretive to FFO from next year, with the REIT partially financing the purchase through an upsized registered public offering of around 2.6 million shares at $96.20 per share. Desert Ridge will add 950 rooms to RHP's portfolio and comes with a higher average daily rate ("ADR") of $349 and a revenue per available room ("RevPAR") of $230 than the other upscale properties in RHP's portfolio. The focus on a niche and high-RevPAR hospitality portfolio, country music entertainment venues, and upscale convention center resorts places RHP as one of the more unique REITs in my portfolio and a source of broad diversification. The depth of coverage and pending Fed rate cuts should aggregate to support a dividend raise next year. The CME FedWatch Tool currently has the probability of 50 basis points of cuts to end 2025 at 86%.

Conclusion

RHP is set to report its third-quarter earnings after the market closes on Monday, November 3, 2025. This should see a full quarter of contributions from a summer of festivals from its entertainment unit, and I expect third-quarter revenue to grow sequentially. The REIT's portfolio is set for strength, but the year-over-year dip in FFO seen during the second quarter and operating expenses that were higher versus their year-ago quarter act to counter bullishness on this. The Gaylord Hotels account for 5 of the top 10 non-gaming hotels in the U.S. by indoor meeting space, and RHP's inclusion of Desert Ridge in this should form an engine for growth. RHP is being rated as a hold.

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters: Short-Lived Hype, Stock Is Too Expensive, Sell
data.description:
Intro

American Eagle Outfitters (NYSE:AEO) is an American lifestyle retailer specializing in casual apparel, footwear, and accessories, primarily for young adults (or those who still feel young). Founded in 1977 by brothers Jerry and Mark Silverman, AEO began as a subsidiary of Retail Ventures, Inc., offering brand-name leisure gear for outdoor pursuits. Rebranded in 1991 amid financial challenges, AEO shifted to private-label merchandise for differentiation. It went public in February 1994, raising funds for growth.

Today, AEO is renowned for brands like American Eagle (core denim and tops), Aerie (intimates and activewear), and Offline by Aerie (athleisure), with over 1,000 global stores blending affordability and trendiness. This year, AEO made headlines through an ad campaign with American actress Sydney Sweeney that was said to have pulled in plenty of new customers over the summer.

However, the initial hype and the stock's massive 25% pop post earnings have reversed. I rate AEO stock a "sell", as the underlying fundamentals are not strong enough to justify the current valuation.

Weaker Fundamentals Than Thought

The long-term chart of AEO shows us we are not dealing with a growing company or a long-term success story.

A price return of 1,196% might sound nice, but this is over 30 years. I doubt the majority of investors have been holding so long. Else, we can see that AEO stock currently is trading on a level where it was in the mid-2000s. Hardly impressive. So basically, 20 years of no returns (excluding the dividend). But plenty of volatility and price fluctuation.

This doesn't mean one cannot make a few bucks with such a stock. But it depends on the entry point. This can be determined at least approximately through a fundamental analysis, which is what we're doing today.

Zooming in, over the last decade, long-term investors would have made only 6% (before dividends), an unsatisfying outcome. As said, in between there can be lucrative entry points in this cyclical company, which is driven by discretionary consumer spending, but also the taste of consumers within the business cycle.

After this foreword, let's examine the company's financial performance. Sales are showing a long-term upward trend. So fine, so good.

Sales growth rates are a bit trickier. Broadly speaking, on average sales are growing somewhere in the low- to mid-single digits with fluctuations. FY 22 was exceptional, as we know, and I would under no circumstances use such a year as a yardstick for future growth assumptions.

Looking now at sales on a rolling-twelve-month quarterly basis, we see the direction is shifting. Sales seem to have peaked for now, as the last few quarters have been showing falling sales. These are all last-twelve-month numbers.

If we now pull out the sales growth rates, we see momentum has shifted to the downside.

We can debate whether this is due to tariffs, more cautious consumers when it comes to discretionary items, both, or something else. But at the moment, there's no growth, and the stock should reflect that, which, for my taste, is not doing (coming back to the valuation later).

Even the guidance from management does not see any growth, despite the hype around the Sydney Sweeney ad and the stock's pop after the last results were released in early April.

Latest management guidance:

Sales are expected to be flat for the year, while gross margins are trending down. Higher sales expenses (SG&A), little surprisingly, but together with tariff-induced higher costs (reflected in gross margins), lead the management to expect operating income of $255–$265 million. This is not a great expected outcome, as I will show now.

First, gross margins. I have to admit, management was able to keep them relatively stable. They have even risen modestly over the last ten years from start to finish.

But with the exception of the crisis years 2020–2021, the expected operating income of $260 million at the midpoint, would be the lowest level over the last rolling decade. Yes, I know, tariff-induced due to higher costs.

But for me, it looks like management tries to keep sales up through expensive ad campaigns. According to them, promotions have been lower thanks to these successful campaigns. But in the end, both are marketing expenses, so that in practice it is from the left pocket to the right pocket.

Anyhow, net income has been rather lumpy in the past. This confirms AEO is not a straight-line growth story.

Taking a look at cash flow, we can see again at best a slow-growth story.

The most important figure for me, free cash flow, gives us even fewer reasons to celebrate.

Only little growth and the business remains very capital intensive, as Capex eats big (and growing) parts of operating cash flow.

You might be wondering, why I am so negative, despite relatively solid looking numbers. I am answering this question now. The reason is the more time passes, the less efficient the company becomes.

For example, returns on assets and capital. Both are trending down. Definitely not positive.

Then, inventory turnover is trending down either. This means, AEO becomes less and less dynamic, with its clothes remaining in stock longer each year. Over the last twelve months, by the way, this figure for the first time dropped below 5x to 4.8x.

This is confirmed by rising average days sales and inventory outstanding. If demand were so strong, these metrics would not be developing as they do.

In other words, the company needs to invest more and more capital to keep the wheel spinning, while efficiency declines. Paired with a lumpy growth outlook in the currently tough environment, the stock should be valued at a discount.

AEO has only a limited amount of debt, which is an advantage. Also, goodwill and intangibles are low, with total equity strongly above these figures. The balance sheet is really strong.

Which leads us to the valuation.

Valuation

Over the last quarters, AEO has aggressively repurchased shares. Especially in the last quarter, management stepped on the gas (likely in anticipation of a successful outcome of their ad campaign).

During the first half, 10% of the stock was bought back. This is very significant.

However, this move has noticeably drained the cash reserves.

In the short term, this looks like a genius move. Below, I framed the period where the buyback likely was conducted. As we can see, at much lower prices. Then the stock popped after the results announcement.

However, AEO stock has already lost a good quarter from its high. This is not making headlines. If the stock dives another 10%, the entire pop that followed the earnings release will be gone. Just to put things into perspective.

Another point is, management spent more than their entire FCF generated over the last twelve months for this buyback. So this was a very aggressive move. But it seems the bullets are fired now. I hope they don't borrow money for another round of buybacks.

The dividend, yielding 3.3%, consumes $90 million annually at current figures. So effectively, AEO generated only $60 million in excess cash after subtracting the dividend from FCF.

I am skeptical, as management isn't expecting to grow this year with cost pressure from tariffs. Okay, they can hike prices to offset or at least counterbalance this a bit. But I doubt consumers will be storming into the stores when prices get jacked up strongly. The current environment is tough to navigate. We have seen, for example, even consumer staples like food producers having issues raising their prices. Clothes are being bought less frequently and are easier to trade down (or wear longer). If they need to be purchased at all, most of us have more in stock than we are wearing. I am not so sure AEO will be able to increase prices strongly without causing side ripple effects in the form of falling demand.

With a FCF of $150 million over the last twelve months and a market cap of $2.6 billion, the multiple is rather steep at 17x. Cash flow was suppressed by negative working capital. Adjusting for that, FCF would be $260 million—a 10x multiple. This sounds more like fair value to me than a dramatic undervaluation.

Where my decision shifted from "hold" to "sell" was a PE ratio comparison. AEO trades for a forward PE of 14x.

For me, this is way too much for this business, despite the recent hype about the ad campaign. In comparison, The GAP (GAP) is trading for a forward PE of 10x, which I deem to be more realistic. The dividend yield is similar, GAP even has a modestly better balance sheet (decent net cash position), significantly more liquidity on hand, firmer margins, and better cash generation. If at all, GAP should trade at a premium, not AEO.

Such apparel retailers should not be bought for more than 10x earnings/FCF, assuming a clean balance sheet. Ideally below that, when they are in a cyclical trough. This is not the case currently. AEO is being priced too optimistically for my taste.

That's why I rate it a "sell", expecting the stock to come back at least to where it was before the pop post Q2 earnings. I fully acknowledge that AEO now has significantly fewer shares outstanding. But please see what the market has been thinking about AEO over the long term. Despite growing sales, the trend for the whole market cap is rather down.

Until the risk and reward does not tilt to a much more favorable setup, I rate AEO a "sell". The risks are too high for me to catch a value trap on its way down in a challenging consumer environment. I view the recent hype as being rather a one-off event than a major turnaround.

Risks To My Thesis

AEO could be acquired by a bigger competitor or taken private, as the market cap is only $2.6 billion, with very little net debt on the balance sheet.

If I am wrong and the recent ad campaign indeed ignited significant new interest in the company and its brands, the business could beat expectations, justifying the current valuation. But this assumes some growth and higher margins with strong and stable cash generation.

Conclusion

I rate AEO a "sell" as the stock looks too expensive for me. Recent hype or not, most of the post-earnings pop has been erased again. The business is becoming less and less efficient over time, making me cautious.
